task: I’m trying to decide whether to have the furnace serviced now or wait until it actually gets colder. What’s the practical call here if I want to avoid a bigger bill later?

turns.agent_response:
Service it **now**.

Practical reason: if it’s going to fail, you want to find out **before** you actually need heat. Waiting until cold weather usually means:
- longer wait times
- higher emergency rates
- parts harder to source
- a small issue turning into a no-heat problem

## Service now if:
- it’s been a year or more since the last check
- it’s making new noises
- the filter has been neglected
- the burner/igniter has been acting up
- you’ve had short cycling, weak airflow, or uneven heat

## You can probably wait if:
- it was serviced recently
- it starts cleanly
- no error codes
- no smell, soot, or strange noise
- airflow and heat have been normal

## What to ask for
A basic tune-up/check should include:
- filter
- burners/ignition
- flame sensor
- blower
- electrical connections
- safety switches
- venting / exhaust check
- thermostat check
- carbon monoxide safety check if applicable

## Red flags
Don’t wait if you notice:
- gas smell
- burning smell
- soot
- repeated breaker trips
- short cycling
- loud rumbling or banging
- visible rust or water near the unit

If you want, I can help you decide based on the **exact symptoms** you’re seeing.

user_simulator.text:
Service it now, yeah. That part is right.

What I’d want next is the cheapest sensible way to do it. Not a sales pitch. Just: what’s the minimum tune-up that actually matters, what can I check myself first, and what are the signs the thing is already past a simple service and needs repair. I don’t need a lecture about comfort. I want to avoid paying emergency rates in January.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — cheapest sensible path is:

## 1) Check yourself first
Before you book anything, do the basic no-cost pass:
- replace the filter
- confirm thermostat settings
- check breaker / service switch
- make sure supply and return vents aren’t blocked
- look for obvious water, soot, rust, or a loose access panel
- note any error code or blinking light

If it starts cleanly after that, you may just need a service, not a repair.

## 2) Minimum tune-up that matters
Ask for a **basic furnace tune-up / safety check**, not a big upsell. The useful parts are:
- filter check/replacement
- burner and ignition check
- flame sensor cleaning/check
- blower inspection
- electrical connection check
- safety switch check
- venting/exhaust check
- thermostat check
- CO/safety check if applicable

That’s the stuff that catches the common failures.

## 3) Signs it’s already beyond a simple service
Call it repair, not tune-up, if you have:
- no heat at all after basic checks
- repeated short cycling
- breaker trips
- gas smell
- burning smell that doesn’t clear
- soot
- loud banging, rumbling, or scraping
- visible rust, corrosion, or water around the unit
- error code that comes back after one reset

## 4) Cheapest practical way to book it
When you call, say:
- “I want a basic furnace tune-up and safety check.”
- “Please quote the service call separately from any repair.”
- “Call me before doing any extra work.”

That keeps them from turning a maintenance visit into a parts parade.

## 5) Best timing
Do it **now**, before cold weather and emergency pricing.

If you want, I can give you a **one-minute furnace check list** you can run through before you call.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the useful version.

One-minute check before you pay anybody:
- filter
- thermostat on heat, set high enough
- breaker and service switch
- vents open, returns not blocked
- look for water, rust, soot, loose panel
- note any blinking code

If all that’s clean, then book a basic tune-up/safety check. Not a “whole-house comfort optimization” nonsense package.

Minimum worth paying for:
- burner / ignition
- flame sensor
- blower
- electrical connections
- safety switches
- venting / exhaust
- CO check if the setup calls for it

If it’s tripping the breaker, making banging or scraping noises, showing soot or rust, or giving the same error after a reset, that’s already repair territory. Don’t let them sell you a tune-up when it’s clearly a parts problem.

And yes — get the price for the service call separate from any repair before they touch anything. That part matters.
